summary: Commander of the Continental Army during the Revolutionary War and the first U.S. president, Washington established foundational precedents for the executive office and voluntarily relinquished power after two terms.
defaultPortraitCredit: Emanuel Leutze, Washington Crossing the Delaware, 1851. Oil on canvas, 12 1/3 x 21 1/6 ft., Metropoliatan Museum of Art

portraits.credit: Charles Willson Peale, George Washington in the Uniform of a Colonial in the Virginia Militia, 1772. Oil on canvas, 50 3/8 x 41 in., Washington-Custis-Lee Collection, Washington and Lee University
Charles Willson Peale, Portrait of George Washington, 1776. Oil on canvas, 44 x 38 5/16 in., The Brooklyn Museum
Joseph Wright, The Powell Protrait of George Washington, 1784. Oil on canvas, 93 x 58 1/2 in., Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts
Robert Edge Pine, George Washington, 1785. Oil on canvas, 35 1/2 x 27 1/2 in., National Portrait Gallery, Smithsonian Institution
Gilbert Stuart, George Washington, 1803. Oil on canvas, 29 x 24 in., Clark Art Institute
Gilbert Stuart, George Washington (Lansdowne Portrait), 1796. Oil on canvas, 97 1/2 x 62 1/2 in., National Portrait Gallery, Smithsonian Institution
Gilbert Stuart, George Washington (The Constable-Hamilton Portrait), 1797. Oil on canvas, 50 x 40 in., The Crystal Bridges Museum of American Art

summary: A leading advocate for independence and the nation's first vice president, Adams steered the young republic through the Quasi-War with France while defending a strong central government.
defaultPortraitCredit: John Trumbull, Declaration of Independence, 1818. Oil on canvas, 12 ft × 18 ft, United States Capital Rotunda

portraits.credit: Benjamin Blyth, John Adams, c. 1766. Pastel on paper, 22 7/16 x 17 1/2 in., Massachusetts Historical Society
John Singleton Copley, John Adams, 1783. Oil on canvas, 93 7/10 × 58 in., Harvard Art Museums, Accession H74
Mather Brown, John Adams, 1788. Oil on canvas, 35 1/2 x 28 1/16 in., National Portrait Gallery, Smithsonian Institution
John Trumbull, John Adams, c. 1792. Oil on canvas, 30 x 24 in., White House Collection / White House Historical Association
John Trumbull, John Adams, 1793. Oil on canvas, 25 x 21 in., National Portrait Gallery, Smithsonian Institution
Gilbert Stuart, John Adams, c. 1800. Oil on canvas, 29 x 24 in., National Gallery of Art
Samuel Finley Breese Morse, Portrait of John Adams, c. 1816. Oil on canvas, 29 3/4 x 24 15/16 in., The Brooklyn Museum, Gift of Harriet H. White
Gilbert Stuart, John Adams, c. 1821. Oil on wood, 26 x 21 7/16 in., National Gallery of Art, Ailsa Mellon Bruce Fund

Gilbert Stuart, John Adams, 1826. Oil on canvas, 30 x 25 in., Smithsonian American Art Museum, Adams-Clement Collection, Gift of Mary Louisa Adams Clement in memory of her mother, Louisa Catherine Adams Clement

summary: Principal author of the Declaration of Independence, Jefferson doubled the nation's size through the Louisiana Purchase and championed agrarian democracy and limited government.
defaultPortraitCredit: John Trumbull, Declaration of Independence, 1818. Oil on canvas, 12 x 18 ft., United States Capitol Rotunda

portraits.credit: Mather Brown, Thomas Jefferson, 1786. Oil on canvas, 28 x 36 in., National Portrait Gallery, Smithsonian Institution
Charles Willson Peale, Thomas Jefferson, 1791. Oil on canvas, Independence National Historical Park, Philadelphia
Charles Peale Polk, Thomas Jefferson, 1799. Oil on canvas, private collection
Rembrandt Peale, Thomas Jefferson, 1800. Oil on canvas, 23 1/8 x 19 1/4 in., White House Collection / White House Historical Association
Rembrandt Peale, Thomas Jefferson, 1805. Oil on linen, 28 x 23 1/2 in., Smithsonian American Art Museum / New-York Historical Society, Gift of Thomas Jefferson Bryan
Matthew Harris Jouett, Thomas Jefferson, c. 1816. Oil on panel, White House Collection
Thomas Sully, Thomas Jefferson, begun 1821, finished 1830. Oil on canvas, 37 1/2 x 32 1/2 in., American Philosophical Society, Philadelphia
John Henri Isaac Browere, Thomas Jefferson, modeled 1825, cast 1940. Bronze, Fenimore Art Museum, Cooperstown, New York, Gift of Stephen C. Clark

summary: Known as the 'Father of the Constitution' for drafting much of its framework, Madison led the nation through the War of 1812 against Great Britain.
defaultPortraitCredit: Walker K. Hancock, James Madison (statue), 1974. Carrara & pink Tennessee marble, U.S. Capitol, Architect of the Capitol

portraits.credit: Charles Willson Peale, James Madison (portrait miniature), 1783. Watercolor on ivory in gold case, Library of Congress
Charles Willson Peale, James Madison, 1792. Oil on canvas, 23 3/16 x 19 1/8 in., Gilcrease Museum
Gilbert Stuart, James Madison, 1804. Oil on canvas, The Colonial Williamsburg Foundation
Gilbert Stuart, James Madison, c. 1805-1807. Oil on canvas, 48 1/2 x 39 3/4 in., Bowdoin College Museum of Art
Thomas Sully (derived from Gilbert Stuart), James Madison, 1809. Oil on panel, 27 1/2 x 19 1/2 in., National Portrait Gallery, Smithsonian Institution

Joseph Wood, James Madison, 1817. Oil on wood, 9 x 7 in., Virginia Historical Society

Charles Bird King(after Joseph Wood), James Madison, 1826. Oil on canvas, U.S. Department of State, Diplomatic Reception Rooms

Asher B. Durand, James Madison, 1833. Oil on canvas, 24 1/4 x 20 1/4 in., New-York Historical Society

summary: Presiding over the 'Era of Good Feelings,' Monroe issued the Monroe Doctrine warning European powers against further colonization of the Americas.
defaultPortraitCredit: Rembrandt Peale, James Monroe, c. 1824-1825. Oil on canvas, James Monroe Museum and Memorial Library

portraits.credit: Emanuel Leutze, Washington Crossing the Delaware (detail, depicting Monroe holding the flag), 1851. Oil on canvas, 149 x 255 in., Metropolitan Museum of Art

Samuel Finley Breese Morse, Portrait of James Monroe, c. 1819. Oil on canvas, 29 1/2 x 24 1/2 in., White House Collection / White House Historical Association
Gilbert Stuart, James Monroe, c. 1820-22. Oil on canvas, 40 1/4 x 32 in., The Metropoliatan Museum of Art
Pendleton's Lithography (after Gilbert Stuart), James Monroe, c. 1828. Lithograph, 11 7/16 x 9 5/8 in., National Portrait Gallery, Smithsonian Institution
Thomas Sully, James Monroe, 1829. Oil on canvas mounted on board, 19 x 15 in., U.S. Department of State, Diplomatic Reception Rooms

summary: A brilliant diplomat and son of John Adams, he promoted national infrastructure and later became a fierce anti-slavery voice in the House of Representatives.

summary: A populist war hero who reshaped the presidency, Jackson dismantled the national bank and forcibly removed Native nations along the Trail of Tears.
